proof_stages.label: Matroid–positroid–polymatroid comparison square
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up matroids, positroids, and polymatroids through bases, rank functions, and root-edge polytopes, emphasizing submodularity and generalized permutohedra as the ambient framework for the later definition.

proof_stages.label: Cryptomorphic definitions of polypositroids
proof_stages.summary: Polypositroids are introduced as polymatroids realizable by consecutive blocks of a totally nonnegative matrix and are then characterized equivalently by m-convex plus sorted-convex bases, cyclic-interval rank inequalities/balanced digraphs, and polytopes with root-parallel edges and twisted-root facet normals.

proof_stages.label: Asymmetric disk metrics as cyclic rank data
proof_stages.summary: Asymmetric metrics, possibly with negative directed distances but no negative directed loops, are related to polypositroids by observing that distances between cyclically ordered boundary points of a disk satisfy exactly the four-point cyclic submodular inequalities.

proof_stages.label: Tropical plabic graphs and reduced cones
proof_stages.summary: Drawing geodesics between boundary marked points converts the disk metric data into shortest-path, i.e. tropical boundary-measurement, data on directed weighted plabic graphs, with reduced graphs requiring chosen geodesics and associated linear inequalities on edge weights.

proof_stages.label: Tropical determinant extension to all ranks
proof_stages.summary: The main formula extends cyclic interval ranks to arbitrary subsets by writing a subset as a union of cyclic intervals and taking a minimum over matchings, equivalently a tropical minor, with uncrossing reducing the relevant terms to noncrossing matchings.

proof_stages.label: Polypositroid fan and tropical-positive Grassmannian structure
proof_stages.summary: The resulting polypositroid fan is identified with the positive tropical GL_n/coordinate-chart piece of the tropical positive Grassmannian, and its cells and faces are described combinatorially by matching ensembles, tropical plabic graphs, noncrossing subdivisions, and related root-polytope language.

examples.name: Crossing matching uncrossing picture
